description:
A suspicious USB-stick has just been imaged and its contents automatically extracted to

        /home/user/investigation/uploads/

    The incident-response run-book says that **every single file dropped into that directory
    has to be hashed, matched against the team’s “known-safe” and “known-malware” databases,
    and the results logged in a very strict format**.

    Your goals:

    1. Create the directory 
          /home/user/investigation/report/
       if it is not already present.

    2.   For every regular file that is currently located in  
             /home/user/investigation/uploads/  
         compute BOTH its     MD5     and     SHA-256     checksums as well as its exact
         size in bytes.

         •  Do the hashing in parallel (e.g. by combining “find” with
            xargs -P 4, GNU parallel, or a similar technique) so the
            workflow scales when the directory contains hundreds of files.
         •  The order of lines in the final log must be **lexicographically
            sorted by the file name** so the SOC’s diff-tools can compare two
            runs quickly.

    3.   Compare the SHA-256 of every file to the two reference lists that the
         IR team maintains:

            /home/user/investigation/reference/known_safe_hashes.txt
            /home/user/investigation/reference/known_malware_hashes.txt

         •  If a SHA-256 hash appears in   
                known_safe_hashes.txt      →  the file’s STATUS is  SAFE  
           If it appears in  
                known_malware_hashes.txt   →  the file’s STATUS is  MALICIOUS  
           If the hash is in neither list  →  the file’s STATUS is  UNKNOWN

    4.   Write a **single line per file** to

            /home/user/investigation/report/checksum_audit.log

         using *exactly* this pipe-separated layout (no extra spaces):

            FILENAME|SIZE_BYTES|MD5|SHA256|STATUS

         Example of the required format (these are only examples – do NOT just
         copy them):

            malloc.c|1839|9838c8f…|1e99b2…|SAFE  
            foobar.iso|0|d41d8cd…|e3b0c4…|MALICIOUS

         Rules to be verified by the grader:
           •  The file is named checksum_audit.log (all lower-case).  
           •  It lives directly inside /home/user/investigation/report/  
           •  Every line follows the five-field, pipe-separated format above.  
           •  Lines are sorted by the first field (FILENAME).  
           •  STATUS is one of: SAFE, MALICIOUS, UNKNOWN (all caps).  

    5.   Produce a very small human-readable summary file

            /home/user/investigation/report/summary.txt

         with the following four lines *exactly* (capitalisation,
         colon-spacing and line-breaks must match):

            Total files scanned: <number>  
            Safe: <number>  
            Malicious: <number>  
            Unknown: <number>

         The numbers must, of course, equal the counts derived from
         checksum_audit.log.

    6.   Everything under /home/user/investigation/ is writable by you; you
         do NOT have, and do not need, root privileges.

    7.   When you are done, the only new artefacts created by your work
         should be:

          •  /home/user/investigation/report/checksum_audit.log  
          •  /home/user/investigation/report/summary.txt

    Remember: the grader will parse both files byte-for-byte, so pay very
    close attention to the required formats.
truth:
<!--
        Initial on-disk state that the automated test harness sets up
        before the agent starts working.
    -->
    <initial_directories>
        /home/user/investigation/
        /home/user/investigation/uploads/
        /home/user/investigation/reference/
    </initial_directories>

    <initial_files>
        <!-- 1. SAFE FILE -->
        <file path="/home/user/investigation/uploads/safe.txt">
hello</file>  <!-- “hello” without a trailing newline -->
        <file path="/home/user/investigation/uploads/malicious.bin"></file> <!-- zero-byte -->
        <file path="/home/user/investigation/uploads/unknown.dat">
test</file>  <!-- “test” without a trailing newline -->

        <!-- reference hash databases -->
        <file path="/home/user/investigation/reference/known_safe_hashes.txt">
2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824
</file>

        <file path="/home/user/investigation/reference/known_malware_hashes.txt">
e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855
</file>
    </initial_files>

    <!-- Cryptographic ground-truth (used by the grader, NEVER shown to the agent) -->
    <ground_truth_hashes>
        <file name="safe.txt" size="5"
              md5="5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592"
              sha256="2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824"
              status="SAFE" />
        <file name="malicious.bin" size="0"
              md5="d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e"
              sha256="e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855"
              status="MALICIOUS" />
        <file name="unknown.dat" size="4"
              md5="098f6bcd4621d373cade4e832627b4f6"
              sha256="9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08"
              status="UNKNOWN" />
    </ground_truth_hashes>

    <!-- Expected contents after the agent finishes -->

    <expected_report_file path="/home/user/investigation/report/checksum_audit.log">
malicious.bin|0|d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e|e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855|MALICIOUS
safe.txt|5|5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592|2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824|SAFE
unknown.dat|4|098f6bcd4621d373cade4e832627b4f6|9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08|UNKNOWN
</expected_report_file>

    <expected_summary_file path="/home/user/investigation/report/summary.txt">
Total files scanned: 3
Safe: 1
Malicious: 1
Unknown: 1
</expected_summary_file>
